THE CITY 
General 
The City was incorporated in 1916 and operates as a general law city.  The City operates under a 
council-manager form of municipal government and is governed by a five-member City Council.  The City is a 
full-service City, offering its residents a wide range of municipal services, including public safety, highways 
and streets, sanitation, water and sewer services, development and public infrastructure improvements, culture 
and leisure programming, and general government functions.  The City encompasses approximately 7.73 
square miles in the San Gabriel Valley in the County of Los Angeles (the “County”), about 8 miles east of 
downtown Los Angeles.  The City is bounded by the City of Los Angeles to the west, the City of Alhambra to 
the north, the City of San Gabriel to the east and the City of Montebello to the South.   
The City supplies potable water to approximately 11,066 single family residential customers, 1,530 
multi-family residential customers, 798 commercial and industrial customers and 213 other customers.  The  
15 
 
Water System serves approximately 95% of the residents and businesses within the City, with private water 
companies serving the remaining portion. 
The City’s main source of water is groundwater pumped from the Main San Gabriel Valley Basin (the 
“Main Basin”), which is a sub -basin of the San Gabriel Valley Basin.  The City also purchases supplemental 
groundwater from the San Gabriel Valley Water Company (“SGVWC”).  See the caption “THE WATER 
SYSTEM—Water Supply.” 
The Water System includes 12 groundwater wells in the Main Basin (of which eight are currently 
active, three are standby wells and one is inactive), approximately 136.6 miles of transmission and distribution 
pipe ranging in size from 2- inches to 36 -inches in diameter, five primary pressure zones, eight closed sub -
zones, 11 active service zone storage reservoirs (with a total storage capacity of approximately 15.8 million 
gallons), two settling tanks, 11 booster pump stations, 20 pressure reducing stations, one imported water 
supply connection with SGVWC and four emergency interconnections. 
Land Use and Service Area 
The Water System’s service area is approximately seven square miles, and is primarily a residential 
community, with 78% of its land zoned for single and multiple family housing.  The remaining portion of the 
City is made up of commercial, employment/technology, mixed uses, open space and public facilities.  As of 
January 1, 2023, the estimated population of the City was approximately 59,288.  Because the City is 
essentially at full build out, it is not expected that the City’s population will increase significantly in the future. 
The Water System provides retail water service to 95% of the City’s residents and businesses within 
the City, with private water companies serving the remaining portion of the City.  The Water System’s service 
area generally coincides with the City’s boundaries with four exceptions: (1) Mark Keppel High School, 
located north of Hellman Avenue and west of New Avenue, is outside the City limits but is served by the 
Water System; (2) in the northwest portion of the City, the area south of the Sheriff’s Academy and west of 
Long Beach Freeway is served by California Water Services Company; (3) in the southwest portion of the 
City, the East Los Angeles Community College and adjacent areas generally south of Floral Drive and west of 
Gerhart Avenue are served by California Water Services Company; and (4) in the east portion of the City, the 
area east of Alisar Avenue and north of Portrero Grande Drive is served by SGVWC. 
Principal employers in the City comprise a diverse group of industries, including healthcare centers 
and big -box retailers.  As of June 30, 2023 the largest principal employers in the City included Garfield 
Medical Center, Southern California Gas Company, Costco, Monterey Park Hospital and Home Depot.  
Additionally, the City recently completed Phase I of The Market Place, the largest development in the City’s 
history which includes nationally and locally recognized tenants such as Home Depot, In -N-Out, Chick-Fil-A 
and Starbucks.  Two additional phases could allow for up to 140,000 square feet of commercial development.  
In April of 2021, a new 288 room Courtyard by Marriott hotel opened along the 10 freeway (the San 
Bernardino Freeway).  Additionally, a new Holiday Inn and Suites development is underway in the City.  The 
Holiday Inn and Suites will be a mixed -use development including hotel rooms, and 85 condominium units, 
and retail uses.  The condominium units were completed in 2023 and are being sold to individual homebuyers.  
The hotel and retail developments are anticipated to be completed in Fiscal Year 2024-25. 
The topography varies throughout the City. The northeast portion of the City is generally flat.  There 
are four separate hilly areas separated by valleys that provide for the major thoroughfares such as Atlantic 
Boulevard, Monterey Pass Road, and the Long Beach Freeway.  The highest elevation in the City is 
approximately 702 feet, located at the easterly end of Sombrero Drive (site of Sombrero Reservoir and Pump 
Station). The lowest elevation is about 260 feet at the southern border of the City along Pomona Boulevard.  
16 
 
Governance and Management 
The City operates under a council- manager form of government.  Council Members are elected by 
districts for four-year, overlapping terms of office. The Mayor, who is selected during each Council 
reorganization every nine and half months, presides over all Council meetings and is the ceremonial head of 
the City for all official functions.  The current members of the City Council and the expiration dates of their 
respective terms are as follows:   
CITY OF MONTEREY PARK 
City Council 
Name District Term Expires 
Thomas Wong, Mayor 1 2026 
Vinh T. Ngo, Mayor Pro-Tem 5 2026 
Yvonne Yiu  2 2024 
Henry Lo  4 2024 
Jose Sanchez  3 2024 
    
Source:  City of Monterey Park. 
The City Council appoints the City Manager who heads the executive branch of the government, 
implements City Council directives and policies and manages the administrative and operational functions 
through the various departmental heads, who are appointed by the City Manager. 
Day-to-day management of the City is delegated to the City Manager, Inez Alvarez.  Prior to being 
appointed to the position of City Manager by the City Council in November of 2023, Ms. Alvarez served as 
the Assistant City Manager of the City of Monterey Park and, prior to that, as the City’s Director of Recreation 
and Community Services. 
Amy Lee serves as the City’s Treasurer.  Ms. Lee was first elected as the City’s Treasurer in 2022.  
Ms. Lee is also an attorney with over 20 years of experience.  Ms. Lee received her law degree from Loyola 
Law School and is currently licensed to practice law in the State of California and is an active member of the 
California State Bar.  Ms. Lee’s law practice is based Monterey Park. 
Martha Garcia serves as the City’s Director of Finance.  Ms. Garcia has over 34 years of experience in 
governmental accounting.  For the first 26 years, Ms. Garcia worked for Kern County where she served the last 
four years as the Lead Accountant over the Human Services Department’s Internal Audit Division.  In 
February of 2016, Ms. Garcia was hired by the City of Tracy as its new Finance Division Manager, where she 
oversaw the day-to-day functions of the division.  Ms. Garcia was hired as Director of Finance for the City of 
Monterey Park in February of 2020. 
Shawn Igoe serves as the City’s Director of Public Works.  Mr. Igoe has been the City’s Public Works 
Director since 2022 and provides oversight to the various Divisions within the Department, including Water, 
Engineering, Parks, Streets, Sewer and Fleet.  Mr. Igoe previously worked for the cities of La Verne, 
Manhattan Beach and Monrovia.  Over his 22 -year career Mr. Igoe has held various positions such as Water 
Supervisor, Utilities Manager and Maintenance Manager.  Mr. Igoe currently holds his State Water Resources 
Control Board, Water Distribution Grade D4 certification and Water Treatment Grade T3 certification. 
Karl H. Berger is a partner with  the law firm of Burke, Williams & Sorensen, LLP and serves as the 
City Attorney.  Mr.  Berger has served as the City Attorney since August  19, 2020.  Mr.  Berger has practiced 
law since 1995 and specializes in the field of public law, including conflicts of interest, open meeting laws, 
public works contracts, redevelopment and successor agencies, personnel matters, all aspects of land use and 
planning laws and CEQA.  
17 
 
Financial Policies 
General.  The City has adopted a comprehensive set of financial policies to serve as a guideline for 
financial matters as further described below.   
General Management and Budget Policies .  The City is required to adopt a balanced budget for its 
General Fund prior to the beginning of the Fiscal Year.  A balanced budget means current revenues are equal 
to current expenditures.  The City’s long -term financial plan takes into account its capital improvement plan 
and financial forecast as part of its expenditure projections, revenue estimates and future debt position.  The 
City is required to avoid budgetary procedures that finance current expenditures at the expense of meeting 
future obligations (e.g., postponing expenditures, accruing future year revenues or rolling over short -term 
debt).   
Revenue Policy.  The City has adopted a revenue policy under which it endeavors to maintain a 
diversified and stable revenue base to minimize the effects of economic fluctuations on revenue accumulation.  
Under the policy the City estimates revenue using objective, analytical processes and conservative projections 
are utilized in cases of assumption uncertainty.  The estimated growth rates used to project revenues are based 
on analysis as adjusted for the City’s experience and outlook as well as State economic conditions.  The City 
funds all current expenditures from current revenues and avoids procedures that balance current budgets by 
postponing needed expenditures, accruing future revenues or rolling over short -term debt.  The City identifies 
basic tax -provided services and establishes user fees and charges for services provided in excess of basic 
services and/or to non-taxpaying users. 
Fund Balance Policy .  The City’s fund balance classification includes five components: 
nonspendable, restricted, committed, assigned, and unassigned.  The City has adopted a fund balance policy 
(the “Fund Balance Policy”) under which the unrestricted General Fund balance, including committed, 
assigned and unassigned must be maintained at a minimum level of two months of the General Fund regular 
revenues or operating expenditures.  The City’s maximum unrestricted fund balance may not exceed 50% of 
General Fund operating expenditures. The General Fund committed fund balance includes amounts for 
Potential Catastrophic Events and Economic Stabilization and the funding of the committed fund balance 
account is reviewed each year during the City’s midyear fiscal review.  The assigned fund balance for future 
capital projects captures accumulated, nonrecurring or unanticipated revenues or expenditures savings and is 
carried forward from year to year to provide resources for the City Council to fund future infrastructure and 
other onetime expenditure needs of the City.  Under the Fund Balance Policy the City maintains an adequate 
reserve to fund annual paid and committed claims in the General Liability Fund and Workers’ Compensation 
Fund, scheduled vehicle maintenance and replacement in the Auto Shop Fund, accrued leave liability in the 
Separation Benefit Fund and technology and telephone replace in the Technology/Data Processing Fund.  The 
City targets a balance in the Enterprise Fund for working capital at a minimum of two months’ annual 
operating expenses. 
Debt Management Policy.  The City has adopted a debt management policy (the “Debt Management 
Policy”) in compliance with California Government Code Section 8855.  The Debt Management Policy 
establishes guidelines and parameters for the effective governance, management and administration of debt 
and other financing obligation issued by the City and related entities with the goal of protecting and enhancing 
the viability of the General Fund and other associated operating funds to enable the City to continue to deliver 
top services to City residents.  The Debt Management Policy is intended to improve the quality of decisions, 
assist with the determination of the structure of debt issuance, identify policy goals and demonstrate a 
commitment to long-term financial planning, including a multi-year capital plan.  Under the Debt Management 
Policy, the City uses debt financing only for capital improvements or projects that cannot be financed from 
current revenues and the project’s useful life or the estimated service life of the equipment must be equal or 
exceed the term of financing.  Debt financing is not considered appropriate for any recurring purpose such as 
current operating and normal maintenance expenditures.    
18 
 
Pension Funding Policy.  The City issued pension obligation bonds in 2021 for the purpose of paying 
the City’s then existing unfunded actuarial liability to CalPERS; however, it is possible that the City may incur 
additional pension liabilities in subsequent years.  The City has implemented a pension funding policy (the 
“Pension Funding Policy”) which addresses future unfunded pension liabilities.  The Pension Funding Policy 
establishes guidelines directing the City Council to contribute more than the required annual payment to 
CalPERS to reduce the accrual of interest, set up a timeline to payoff any new liabilities within a certain 
number of years and refinance the liability at a lower interest rate.  For more information regarding the City’s 
CalPERS plans, see the caption “WATER SYSTEM FINANCIAL INFORMATION—Employees’ Retirement 
Plan—CalPERS.”  
Capital Improvement Program Policy.  The City has adopted a capital improvement program policy 
(the “Capital Improvement Program Policy”) under which the City utilizes a five -year capital improvement 
program to systematically plan, schedule and finance capital projects as determined by the City Council.  The 
five-year program includes major ongoing maintenance and rehabilitation costs to existing infrastructure and 
facilities, as well as the cost of new facilities or capital improvements.  The City’s capital improvement plan 
identifies each proposed project, the year the project will start and the proposed method of financing.  The City 
will actively pursue grant and other outside funding for all capital improvement projects.  The City maintains 
all of its assets at a level adequate to protect the City’s capital infrastructure and to minimize future 
maintenance and replacement costs.  Whenever the City finances capital projects through the issuance of 
bonds, it must repay the bonds within a period not to exceed the expected useful life of the project.  The City 
confines long -term borrowing to capital improvements or projects that cannot be financed from current 
revenues.  For more information regarding future improvements to the Water System, see the caption “THE 
WATER SYSTEM—Future Water System Improvements.”  
The City also adheres to the following additional financial policies: Policy for Disclosures Procedures, 
Investment Policy and OPEB Policy.   
Employees 
As of June  30, 2023, the City had approximately 292 full -time equivalent employees, of which 
6 worked exclusively for the Water System, 26 worked in the Public Works Department with responsibility for 
the Water System and other public works functions and 260 worked in general government services, including 
finance. 
Confidential employees are represented by the Confidential Employees Association (the “CEA”).  
General miscellaneous employees, including those employees working exclusively for the Water System, are 
represented by the Monterey Park Service Employees’ International Union (the “SEIU”) Local 721.  
Managers, including managers in the Water Department, are represented by the Mid -Managers Association 
(the “MMA”).  The City’s labor agreements with CEA, SEIU, and MMA expire on June  30, 2025.  The City 
has never experienced a strike, slowdown or work stoppage. 
Budget Process 
The City’s budget preparations follow a three- phase approach that begins each year with the 
preparation of the midyear budget review report, which includes a comprehensive long -term financial plan and 
establishes preparatory guidelines for the budget.  The Fiscal Year of the City begins on the first day of July of 
each year and ends on the 30th day of June of the following year (each a “Fiscal Year”).  The City Manager 
presents the proposed budget to the City Council for review prior to the beginning of each Fiscal Year and the 
City Council holds public hearings to review and approve the proposed budget.  The final adopted budget, 
reflecting the City Council’s directions, is approved no later than June 30 of each year.  The budgeting process 
generally spans an approximately four-month timeframe beginning in mid-February of each year.  
19 
 
Legal budgetary control for operating budgets is exercised at the department and fund level.  The City 
is required to adopt a balanced budget for its General Fund at the beginning of the Fiscal Year.  Formal 
budgetary integration and encumbrance accounting are employed as management control devices during the 
year for all funds. 
The City Manager is responsible for preparing and submitting to the City Council the annual budget 
for the operations of the City for each Fiscal Year. The annual budget adopted by the City Council serves as a 
guideline for operations of the City.  From the effective date of the budget, the date of formal adoption by the 
City Council, expenditure amounts as proposed are appropriated to departments and offices for the respective 
expenditure objects and purposes named or described.  Budget expenditures are controlled at the fund and the 
department level.  The City Manager is authorized to transfer appropriations between the accounts of any 
department within individual funds.  Council approval is required only for transfers between funds, or an 
increase in total appropriations. 
The City Manager presents a midyear fiscal (budget) review (the “Midyear Review”) which provides 
a written analysis of the City’s financial health.  The Midyear Review is an update for significant events and 
charges occurring since the last update in the previous year.  As part of the Midyear Review, all of the key 
assumptions underlying the current budget are reviewed and revised as necessary.  Appropriated amounts are 
as originally adopted or as adjusted by the City Council throughout the year.   
The budget is formally integrated into the accounting system and employed as a management control 
device during the year for all governmental funds.  At Fiscal Year -end, unexpended and unencumbered budget 
appropriations lapse.  Encumbrances outstanding at Fiscal Year -end are carried into the following Fiscal Year 
for continuing appropriation.  
Under Article XIIIB of the California Constitution, the City is restricted as to the amount of annual 
appropriations, and if certain proceeds of taxes exceed allowed appropriations, the excess must either be 
refunded to the State Controller or refunded to the taxpayers through revised tax rate or revised fee schedules. 
For the Fiscal Year ended June 30, 2023 , based on calculations by the City, proceeds of taxes did not exceed 
the appropriations limit. 
The City Council adopted the budget for Fiscal Year 2023-24 on June 21, 2023. 
City Insurance 
The City is exposed to various risks of losses related to torts, theft of, damage to and destruction of 
assets, errors and omissions, and natural disasters.  The City is partially self -insured for workers’ 
compensation, general liability, and automobile liability.  The City is fully self -insured for unemployment 
insurance claims (reimbursable account with Employment Development Department).  The Workers’ 
Compensation Internal Service Fund and the General Liability Internal Service Fund were established to 
account for the collection of premiums from various City departments related to the City’s insurance and self -
insurance programs. 
The City retains the risk for the first $500,000 of each Workers’ Compensation claim and is a member 
of PRISM, for coverage up to $5,000,000 per occurrence.  The City has also purchased excess workers’ 
compensation insurance up to statutory limits.  The City retains the risk for the first $1,000,000 of each 
General Liability and Automobile Liability claim and claims that exceed the City’s self -insurance limit are 
insured through PRISM for coverage up to $25 million per occurrence.  The City also purchases insurance 
through PRISM for other risks of loss, including property loss, emergency vehicle physical damage, cyber, and 
special events.  The City’s property insurance currently includes flood and earthquake coverage (although such 
coverage is not required to be maintained by the Installment Purchase Agreement) and covers certain facilities 
in the Water System, but not, for example, underground pipelines.  
20 
 
PRISM is comprised of around 300 public entities through out the State of California.  The 
comprehensive general liability insurance includes monetary damages for personal liability, property damage, 
and public officials’ errors and omissions.  PRISM excess insurance premiums are based on losses incurred by 
the insured, the risk pool entities, and current market for general liability coverages. 
There have been no significant changes in insurance coverage as compared to Fiscal Year 2022 -23 
and the City has not paid out any settlement in excess of the applicable insurance coverage during the past 
three Fiscal Years.  Estimates for liabilities have been accrued in the Workers’ Compensation and General 
Liability Internal Service Funds.  These funds also include an estimate for incurred but not reported claims. 
At June 30, 2023 total estimated claims payable were as follows.  
General Liability  $ 9,386,265 
Workers’ Compensation   14,509,962 
 Total  $ 23,896,227 
    
Source:  City of Monterey Park Audited Financial Statements for Fiscal Year 2022-23. 
No assurance can be given as to the adequacy of the insurance maintained now or in the future by the 
City to fund necessary repairs or replacement of any portion of the Water System, and the City does not have 
any obligation under the Installment Purchase Agreement to maintain earthquake coverage or other coverage 
in the current coverage amounts.  Significant damage to the Water System could affect the City’s ability to 
generate sufficient Net Revenues to pay the Bonds.  See the caption “CERTAIN RISK TO 
BONDHOLDERS—Natural Disasters.”  
The City has not settled any claims that exceeded its insurance coverage in the past three years. 
See Appendix B under the caption “INSTALLMENT PURCHASE AGREEMENT —COVENANTS 
OF THE CITY—Insurance” for a description of insurance coverages that are required to be maintained while 
the Bonds are outstanding.

The City has no outstanding obligations that are payable from Net Revenues of the Water System on a 
senior or parity basis to the Series 2024 Installment Payments.

Lease”), the proceeds of which were used to finance various energy efficiency improvements and upgrades, 
including replacement water meters. The City pays approximately 85% of the Siemens Lease payments from 
Net Revenues; however the City’s obligation to make payments on the Siemens Lease is not secured by a 
pledge of such Net Revenues.  The City’s obligation under the Siemens Lease matures in June 2030.

of $400,000 from Revenues ; repayment is currently expected to be gin in Fiscal Year 2024 -25 (see Table 14) .  
The 2021 Loan Agreement does not bear interest so long as the City makes all required payments when due.   
21 
 
The City’s obligation to make payments under the 2021 Loan Agreement is an unsecured obligation of the 
City payable from Revenues on a subordinate basis to the Series 2024 Installment Payments.

basis with the Series 2024 Installment Payments in the future upon satisfaction of the conditions that are

Purchase Agreement and described under the caption “SECURITY FOR THE BONDS — Additional Parity

Audited Financial Statements 
A copy of the most recent audited financial statements of the City prepared by The Pun Group LLP, 
Santa Ana, California (the “Auditor”) is set forth in Appendix A.  The Auditor’s letter dated February 15, 2024 
is located at the beginning of the Financial Section therein.  The Auditor has not undertaken to update the 
audited financial statements of the City or its report, or to take any action intended or likely to elicit 
information concerning the accuracy, completeness or fairness of the statements made in this Official 
Statement, and no opinion is expressed by the Auditor with respect to any event subsequent to its report dated 
December 30, 202 3.  See Appendix A  — “CITY OF MONTEREY PARK COMPREHENSIVE AUDITED 
FINANCIAL REPORT FOR THE FISCAL YEAR ENDED JUNE 30, 2023.” 
The summary operating results that are contained under the caption “WATER SYSTEM FINANCIAL 
INFORMATION—Historical Operating Results and Debt Service Coverage” are derived from these financial 
statements and audited financial statements for prior Fiscal Years (excluding certain non -cash items and after 
certain other adjustments), and are qualified in their entirety by reference to such statements, including the 
notes thereto. 
The City maintains its accounting records in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting 
Principles (“GAAP”) and the standards established by the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (the 
“GASB”).  A complete set of financial statements is prepared annually for the City Council and City staff in a 
Annual Comprehensive Financial Report for the City. 
The government-wide financial statements are designed to provide information of the City’s financial 
activities in a manner similar to a private-sector company. Government-wide financial statements consist of the 
statement of net position and the statement of activities, which are prepared using the economic resources 
measurement focus and the accrual basis of accounting. These statements provide both short- term and long-
term information about the City’s financial position, which is helpful in assessing the City’s economic 
condition at the end of the fiscal year. 
The statement of net position presents all of the City’s financial resources along with its capital assets 
and long-term obligations. The difference between the assets and the liabilities is reported as the net position. 
The statement of activities reports the gross and net costs of various activities carried out by the City 
and how the City’s net position changed during the fiscal year. This statement summarizes the cost of 
providing services by the City and includes all current year revenues and expenses. 
Both government -wide financial statements distinguish functions of the City that are principally 
supported by taxes and intergovernmental revenues (i.e. governmental activities) from other functions that are 
intended to recover all or a significant portion of their costs through user fees and charges (i.e. business -type 
activities). The governmental activities of the City include general government, public safety, community 
development, culture and recreation, highways and streets, health, and debt service.  The business -type  
22 
 
activities reflect the City’s Water System operations, waste management activities, and sanitary sewer 
activities. 
The government -wide financial statements include not only the City but also those legally separate 
entities for which the City is financially accountable.  These entities include the Monterey Park Housing 
Authority and the Monterey Park Financing Authority.  Financial information for these component units is 
reported as an integral part of the City. 
The City accounts for the Water System in the Water Utility Enterprise Fund.  The City’s Water 
Utility Enterprise Fund is a proprietary fund used to account for operations that are financed and operated in a 
manner similar to private business enterprises and where the intent of the City is that the costs (expenses, 
including depreciation) of providing goods and services to the general public on a continuing basis be financed 
or recovered primarily through user charges.  Proprietary funds distinguish operating revenues and expenses 
from nonoperating items.  Operating revenues and expenses generally result from providing services and 
producing and delivering goods in connection with a proprietary fund’s principal ongoing operations.  The 
principal operating revenues of the Water Utility Enterprise Fund, Refuse Enterprise Fund, Sewer Enterprise 
Fund, and of the government’s internal service funds are charges to customers for sales and services.  
Operating expenses for enterprise funds and internal service funds include the cost of sales and services, on 
capital assets. All revenues and expenses not meeting this definition are reported as nonoperating revenues and 
expenses. 
Single Audit Report 
As a recipient of federal funds, the City is required to undergo an audit in accordance with the Single 
Audit Act of 1996 and the U.S. Office of Management and Budget Circular A -133.  In its Single Audit of 
Federally Assisted Grant Programs for Fiscal Year 2022 -23 prepared by the Auditor and attached hereto as 
Appendix F (the “Single Audit Report”), the Auditor found certain deficiencies in internal control over 
compliance that the Auditor considered to be “significant deficiencies,” as defined in the Auditor’s letter dated 
March 28, 2024, which accompanies the Single Audit Report.  
The Single Audit Report notes that, during the Fiscal Year 2022 -23 audit, the Auditor noted the 
certain conditions that in resulted adjustments to the financial statements, including prior period adjustments 
(1) to correct the understated deferred outflow of pension amount at July 1, 2022 (Approximately $1.3 
million), (2)  to correct the beginning unavailable revenue and fund balance at July 1, 2022 of the Housing 
Special Revenue Fund (Approximately $1.19 million), (3)  to reallocate the beginning balance of the pension 
related balances between government activities and business -type activities (Approximately $827,000), (4)  to 
reallocated the beginning balance of the other postemployment benefits related balances between government 
activities and business-type activities (Approximately $1.2 million), and (5) to correct the allocation of interest 
payable on pension obligation bonds into internal service funds based on the bonds’ allocation.  In response to 
this finding, the City plans to implement a monthly reporting cycle which will include review of internal 
control over financial reporting on a monthly basis. If and when errors are detected, journal entries will be 
prepared monthly to correct the financial reports.  The City also plans to implement ongoing training for 
finance department staff. 
The Los Angeles County Safe, Clean Water Program Implementation Ordinance and the Transfer 
Agreement between the City and the Los Angeles County Flood Control District relating to the Safe, Clean 
Water Program requires the City to prepare an annual expenditure plan and annual report.  The Auditor noted 
two instances in which the City’s submission was late – the Annual Spending Plan for Fiscal Year 2022 -23 
was submitted six days late on April 7, 2022, and the Annual Progress/Expenditure Report for Fiscal Yea r 
2022-23 was submitted approximately six weeks late on February 14, 2024.  The Auditor noted that, without 
timely submitting the required annual spending plan and the annual reports, the City is not in compliance with 
the Safe Clean Water Program requirements and could potentially lose the funding.   The City received 
approximately $727,939 in Fiscal Year 2020 -21, approximately $737,121 in Fiscal Year 2021- 22, and  
23 
 
approximately $738,669 in Fiscal Year 2022 -23 under the Safe Clean Water Program.   These amounts are 
required to be used for stormwater projects and related costs do not constitute Revenues of the Water System. 
The City is required to comply with a System for Award Management (“SAM”) in making subawards 
of federal funds.  The Single Audit Report notes that for two out of five samples selected for testing, the City 
did not have documentation verifying the vendors against the SAM prior to entering into contracts with the 
vendors to ensure that they were not suspended or debarred from federally funded purchases.  If the City does 
not verify whether vendors are suspended or debarred from working on federally funded projects, the City 
could contract with vendors that are prohibited from working on federally funded projects and potentially lose 
funding.   
The Single Audit Report has been included herein in reliance upon the report of the Auditor.  The 
Auditor has not undertaken to update the Single Audit Report, or to take any action intended or likely to elicit 
information concerning the accuracy, completeness or fairness of the statements made in this Official

THE WATER SYSTEM  
General 
The City supplies potable water to approximately 11,066 single family residential customers, 1,530 
multi-family residential customers, 798 commercial and industrial customers and 213 other customers.  See the 
caption “THE CITY—Land Use and Service Area.”  
The City’s main source of water is groundwater pumped from the Main San Gabriel Valley Basin (the 
“Main Basin”), which is a sub -basin of the San Gabriel Valley Basin.  The City also purchases supplemental 
groundwater from the San Gabriel Valley Water Company (“SGVWC”).  See the caption “THE WATER 
SYSTEM—Water Supply.” 
The Water System includes 12 groundwater wells in the Main Basin (of which eight are currently 
active, three are standby wells and one is inactive), approximately 136.6 miles of transmission and distribution 
pipe ranging in size from 2- inches to 36 -inches in diameter, five primary pressure zones, eight closed sub -
zones, 11 active service zone storage reservoirs (with a total storage capacity of approximately 15.8 million 
gallons), two settling tanks, 11 booster pump stations, 20 pressure reducing stations, one imported water 
supply connection with SGVWC and four emergency interconnections. 
Water Supply 
The City’s sources of water are groundwater pumped from the Main Basin through the City’s eight 
active groundwater wells and local groundwater purchased from SGVWC.  See the captions “—Historical 
Water System Supply” and “ —Projected Water System Supply” for historical and projected information with 
respect to the City’s sources of water supply. 
Groundwater.  The City’s main source of water for the Water System is groundwater pumped from 
the Main Basin, which is a sub -basin of the San Gabriel Valley Basin pursuant to California Department of 
Water Resources (“DWR”) Bulletin 118, Basin Number 4 -013.  Pursuant to the Sustainable Groundwater 
Management Act of 2014 (“SGMA”), the Main Basin was named as an adjudicated groundwater basin and is 
exempt from the requirements of developing a Groundwater Sustainability Plan and subsequently was 
designated a very-low- priority basin in DWR’s 2019 SGMA Basin Prioritization report.  In compliance with 
SGMA, the Main Basin Watermaster (as defined below) submits its Annual Report to DWR.  
24 
 
The Main Basin lies in the heart of the San Gabriel Valley and has an estimated total fresh water 
storage capacity of approximately 9.5 million acre- feet.  The Main Basin is replenished by stream runoff from 
the adjacent mountains and hills, by rainfall directly on the surface, subsurface inflow from Raymond Basin 
and Puente Basin, return flow from water applied for overlying uses, and imported water replenishment .  The 
Main Basin is bounded by the San Gabriel Mountains to the north, the San Rafael and Merced Hills on the 
west, the Puente Hills and the San Jose Hills on the south, and a low divide between the San Gabriel River 
system and the Upper Santa Ana River system on the east.  Only the northerly portion of the City overlies the 
Main Basin and, therefore, all of the City’s wells are located in the adjacent City of Rosemead directly 
overlying the Main Basin. 
In the 1940s, the San Gabriel Valley experienced rapid urbanization and an increase in population that 
led to increased demand for water.  By the 1950s, the Main Basin was put into a state of overdraft due to the 
increase in water consumption and periods of drought.  The water production from the Main Basin exceeded 
the amount that was replaced.  Downstream parties became especially concerned and legal action was initiated 
on their behalf, resulting in a court decision requiring the water uses in the area upstream of Whittier Narrows 
(the “Upper Area”) to guarantee a source of water to water users of the area downstream of Whittier Narrows 
(the “Lower Area”).  Whittier Narrows is a low gap between the Merced and Puente Hills, just northwest of the 
City of Whittier, through which the San Gabriel River and the Rio Hondo flow to the coastal plain of Los 
Angeles County. 
Initially, the Upper San Gabriel Valley Municipal Water District was responsible for the Upper Area’s 
obligations to the Lower Area.  However, due to the fact that the Upper San Gabriel Valley Municipal Water 
District boundaries did not encompass the entire Main Basin, a complaint was filed that would adjudicate 
water rights in the Main Basin and bring all producers under control of one governing body.  This led to the 
Main Basin Judgment in 1973. 
The Long Beach Judgment, entered in September of 1965, divides the supply of the San Gabriel River 
system at Whittier Narrows, between San Gabriel Valley upstream and the coastal plain of Los Angeles 
County downstream.  Under the Long Beach Judgment, the Lower Area is to receive a quantity of usable water 
annually from the San Gabriel River system comprised of usable surface flow, subsurface flow at Whittier 
Narrows and water exported to the Lower Area.  A provision is made for the supply of make- up water by the 
Upper Area for years in which the guaranteed entitlement is not received by the Lower Area.  The entitlement 
of the Lower Area varies annually, depending on the 10 -year average annual rainfall in San Gabriel Valley for 
the 10 years ending in the entitlement year.  The San Gabriel River Watermaster, a separate watermaster from 
the Main Basin Watermaster, assures compliance with the Long Beach Judgment. 
Under the Main Basin Judgment, entered in January of 1973, all rights to the diversion of surface 
water and production of groundwater within the Main Basin and its relevant watershed were adjudicated.  The 
judgment provides administration of the provisions of the judgment by the Main San Gabriel Basin 
Watermaster (the “Main Basin Watermaster”).  The Main Basin Watermaster is a nine- member board which 
administers the water rights and manages the water quality and supply of the Main Basin.  The Main  Basin 
Watermaster operates under a formal set of rules and regulations that define the procedures by which the Main 
Basin is operated and managed.  Water producers must obtain Main Basin Watermaster approval for 
constructing or modifying a well, constructing a groundwater treatment plant, increasing groundwater 
extraction, spreading water in the basin, and spreading and storing supplemental water under a cyclic storage 
agreement. 
The Main Basin Judgment does not restrict the quantity of water which parties may extract from the 
Main Basin.  Rather, the Main Basin Judgment provides a means for replacing all annual extractions in excess 
of a producer’s annual right to extract water with supplemental imported water.  Each year the Main Basin 
Watermaster reviews water supply conditions including local rainfall, groundwater levels, local stormwater 
runoff available for replenishment, imported water availability and the amount of imported water stored in the 
Main Basin for future demands and establishes an “Operating Safe Yield.” The Operating Safe Yield is the  
25 
 
amount of groundwater that can safely be extracted for the succeeding fiscal year before more expensive 
imported water would need to be purchased to replenish the Main Basin for all production in excess of the 
water rights.  Regardless of the Operating Safe Yield adopted there is never a restriction on the amount of 
water which may be pumped from the Main Basin, only the cost of producing the groundwater is impacted.  
The Main Basin Watermaster is not restricted as to when or how much untreated imported water be delivered 
to the Main Basin, only that it ultimately be delivered.  In addition, the City has established an untreated 
imported water (cyclic) storage account in the Main Basin which the City may draw upon to offset its potential 
future production in excess of its water rights.  In doing so, the City reduces its need to purchase untreated 
imported water in the future in the midst of a drought when imported water supplies may be limited. 
Each water producer, including the City, is allocated its portion of the Operating Safe Yield.  If a 
water producer extracts more than its allocated portion, it must pay a Replacement Water Assessment that is 
sufficient to purchase one acre-foot of supplemental water to be spread in the basin for each acre-foot of excess 
production.  The City has prescriptive right to 3.39216 percent of the Operating Safe Yield.  Over the last ten 
fiscal years, the average Operating Safe Yield has been an average of 150,000 acre- feet per year and therefore 
the City’s average entitlement share over such period has been approximately 5,088.24 acre-feet per year. 
The City is a sub -agency of San Gabriel Valley Municipal Water District (“San Gabriel District”), 
which is a wholesale water agency with responsibility for delivering supplemental water to the Main Basin on 
behalf of the City and the cities of Alhambra, Azusa and Sierra Madre.  San Gabriel District has a contract 
with the State of California Department of Water Resources for the purchase of water from the State Water 
Project, which water San Gabriel District delivers directly to the spreading grounds in the Main Basin.  When 
necessary to replenish the Main Basin, the Main Basin Watermaster purchases water from San Gabriel District 
for replenishment of the Main Basin. 
See the captions “—Historical Water System Supply” and “—Projected Water System Supply” for 
more information with respect to the Water System’s historical and projected water supply from local 
groundwater in the Main Basin. 
Purchased Local Groundwater .  The City can purchase local groundwater from SGVWC.  The City 
has a 10 -inch connection with SGVWC, with a capacity of approximately 4,000 gallons per minute (gpm).  
The City purchases local groundwater from SGVWC as a supplemental water supply source during periods 
when the City’s own water system is down due to maintenance or emergency repair.  Over the past five years, 
the City’s annual water purchases from SGVWC have ranged from 10 acre -feet to 2000 acre-feet, as shown in 
Table 1.  The City generally purchases water from SGVWC when several of the City’s wells cannot operate 
either due to water contamination or maintenance.  See the caption  “THE WATER SYSTEM—Water Quality 
and Treatment—City Water Treatment Facilities ” for a discussion of imported water purchases in Fiscal Years 
2022-23 and 2023-24 during the design, construction and testing of the CGTS facility. 
See the captions “—Historical Water System Supply” and “—Projected Water System Supply” for 
more information with respect to the Water System’s historical and projected water supply from local 
groundwater purchased from SGVWC. 
Emergency Connections.  The City also has four interconnections with the City of Montebello, the 
Golden State Water Company, Metropolitan Water District of Southern California and the California Water 
Service Company for emergency purposes only.  The City has not used such interconnections in the past. 
Water Quality and Treatment 
General.  During the late 1970s and early 1980s significant groundwater contamination was 
discovered in the San Gabriel Valley Basin.  The contamination was caused in part by past practices of local 
industries that had carelessly disposed of industrial solvents referred to as Volatile Organic Compounds 
(“VOCs”) as well as by agricultural operations that infiltrated nitrates into the groundwater.  Further  
26 
 
investigation revealed widespread VOC contamination significantly impacting the basin.  This discovery led 
the United States Environmental Protection Agency (the “EPA”) to place four portions of the San Gabriel 
Valley Basin, including the portion designated as the South El Monte Operable Unit (where all of the City’s 
groundwater wells are located), on the National Priorities List under authority of Comprehensive 
Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act of 1980 (“CERCLA”), also known as the 
Superfund program.  CERCLA requires parties responsible for pollution to comply with the process of 
carrying out federal cleanup orders.  Cleanup efforts were undertaken at the local, state, and federal level.   See 
the caption “—EPA Settlement ” below. 
Local water agencies adopted a joint resolution in 1989 regarding water quality issues that stated Main 
Basin Watermaster should coordinate local activities aimed at preserving and restoring the quality of 
groundwater in the Main Basin.  The joint resolution also called for a cleanup plan.  In 1991, the Court granted 
Main Basin Watermaster the authority to control pumping for water quality purposes.  Accordingly, Main 
Basin Watermaster added Section 28 to its Rules and Regulations regarding water quality management.  The 
new responsibilities included development of a Five -Year Water Quality and Supply Plan, updating it 
annually, submitting it to the California Regional Water Quality Control Board, Los Angeles Region, and 
making it available for public review by November 1 of each year. 
The Main Basin Watermaster prepares and annually updates the Five- Year Water Quality and Supply 
Plan in accordance with the requirements of the Section 28 Rules and Regulations.  The objective is to 
coordinate groundwater-related activities so that both water supply and water quality in the Main Basin are 
protected and improved.  Many important issues are detailed in the Five- Year Plan, including how Main Basin 
Watermaster plans to: monitor groundwater supply and quality; develop projections of future groundwater 
supply and quality; ensure adequate supplemental water is available for groundwater replenishment; review 
and cooperate on cleanup projects, and provide technical assistance to other agencies; assure that pumping 
does not lead to further degradation of water quality in the Main Basin; address Perchlorate, N -
nitrosodimethylamine (“NDMA”), and other emerging contaminants in the Main Basin; develop a cleanup and 
water supply program consistent with the EPA’s plans for its San Gabriel Valley Basin Superfund sites; and 
coordinate and manage the design, permitting, construction, and performance evaluation of the Baldwin Park 
Operable Unit cleanup and water supply plan. 
The Main Basin Watermaster, in coordination with the Upper San Gabriel Valley Municipal Water 
District, has worked with state and federal regulators and local water companies to clean up water supplies.  
Section 28 of the Main Basin Watermaster’s Rules and Regulations require all producers (including the City) 
to submit an application to 1) construct a new well, 2) modify an existing well, 3) destroy a well, or 4) 
construct a treatment facility.  The Main Basin Watermaster prepares a report on the implications of the 
proposed activity.  As a party to the Main Basin Judgment, the City reviews a copy of these reports and is 
provided the opportunity to submit comments on the proposed activity before the Main Basin Watermaster 
Board takes final action. 
The San Gabriel Water Quality Authority (“WQA”) was established by the State Legislature on 
February 11, 1993 to develop, finance and implement groundwater treatment programs in the San Gabriel 
Valley Basin, including the sub-basins such as the Main Basin.  Section 406 of the act which created the WQA 
(the “WQA Act”) requires the WQA “to develop and adopt a basinwide groundwater quality management and 
remediation plan” (which is referred to as the “Section 406 Plan”) that is required to be consistent with the 
EPA’s National Contingency Plan (“NCP”) and Records of Decision (“ROD”) and all requirements of the Los 
Angeles Regional Water Quality Control Board (“LARWQCB”).  According to the WQA Act, the Section 406 
Plan, which is incorporated into the City’s Urban Water Management Plan, must include: characterization of 
basin contamination; a comprehensive cleanup plan; strategies for financing the design, construction, operation 
and maintenance of groundwater cleanup facilities; provision for a public information program; and 
coordination of activities with federal, state, and local entities.  
27 
 
WQA reviews and adopts the Section 406 Plan on an annual basis and as necessary, makes revisions 
according to changing regulatory, political and/or funding environments.  In support of the Section 406 Plan, 
WQA also adopts an annual budget which includes all projects (actual or planned) WQA is facilitating through 
its participation during that time period.  The budget identifies the various funding sources, and combinations 
thereof, to ensure full funding for each can be achieved. 
City Water Treatment Facilities .  The City has installed treatment facilities to meet all applicable 
standards for drinking water and the City operates three separate treatment facilities.  In 2016, City 
commenced the design of a new Centralized Groundwater Treatment System (the “CGTS”), which ties 
together Well s No. 5, No.  9, No.  12, and No.  15 and utilizes an Ultraviolet/Advanced Oxidation Process 
(UV/AOP) and Liquid Phase Granular Activated Carbon (“LGAC”) to treat VOC and PFAS/PFOA at these 
well sites.  In late 2020, trace levels of PFAS were discovered, prompting the State Water Resources Control 
Board – Division of Drinking Water (DDW) to request the City to incorporate a PFAS/PFOA removal system 
into the CGTS. Responding to this additional concern, the City initiated the changes and updated the design 
and construction to include PFAS/PFOA removal in 2021.  After approximately seven and a half years, the 
CGTS with the PFAS/PFOA removal component was completed in May 2023 with approval from DDW to 
operate the system. 
Rigorous CGTS system testing, and water quality analysis were conducted to ensure the system ’s 
efficiency and attain the required permitting by DDW.  During construction and testing of the CGTS 
modifications required to address the PGAS/PFOA treatment requirements, the City’s regular production wells 
were unusable.  During this time, the City was able to meet system demands by purchasing imported water 
through an interconnection with SGVMWC.  This additional cost was significantly higher than well water, 
approximately $183,652 in Fiscal Year 2021 -22, $1,028,170 in Fiscal Year 2022- 23, and $1,840,160 in Fiscal 
Year 2023-24.  See Tables 1, 9, 13 and 14.  With the CGTS now online, the City does not expect to purchase 
imported water in the upcoming Fiscal Years.  See Tables 9 and 14.   
Wells No.  1, No.  10 and the Fern well flow to an LGAC treatment facility at the Delta Treatment 
Plant for VOC treatment.  Production from Wells No.  12 and No. 15 are operated consistently with the South 
El Monte Operable Unit Record of Decision. 
PFAS.  In 2019, the State of California Water Resources Control Board’s Division of Drinking Water 
(the “Division ”) lowered the Notification Levels (the “NLs ”) for Perfluorooctanoic acid (“ PFOA”) and 
Perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (“PFOS”) to 5.1 and 6.5 parts per trillion (“ PPT”), respectively.  NLs are non -
regulatory, precautionary health-based measures for concentrations of chemicals in drinking water that warrant 
notification and further monitoring and assessment.  In 2020, the Division lowered the Response Level (the 
“RL”) for PFOA and PFOS from 70 PPT, combined, to 10 and 40 PPT, respectively.   RLs are non-regulatory, 
precautionary health-based measures that are set at higher levels than NLs and represent thresholds at which 
the Division recommends that water systems remove a water source from use or treat it. 
In addition, on March 14, 2023, Maximum Contaminant Levels (MCL) of 4 PPT for PFOA and PFOS 
and cumulative limits for certain other chemicals which are described below were proposed by the EPA.  On 
April 10, 2024 the EPA announced that the proposed MCLs would be enforceable rules. 
PFOA and PFOS are fluorinated organic chemicals which are part of a family of synthetic compounds 
referred to as per - and polyfluoroalkyl substances (“ PFAS”).  PFAS are water and lipid resistant substances 
that were previously used in a variety of manufacturing processes and industrial applications.  They are often 
present in water supplies which are impacted by wastewater treatment plant effluent or active or former 
military installations.  Recent technological advances have enabled water agencies to detect PFAS compounds 
at very low concentrations. 
In addition to PFOS and PFOA, the Division tasked the California Office of Environmental Health 
Hazard Assessment with evaluating and recommending NLs for the following additional PFAS compounds on  
28 
 
February 6, 2020: perfluorohexane sulfonic acid (known as PFHxS), perfluorobutane sulfonic acid (known as 
PFBS), perfluorohexanoic acid (known as PFHxA), perfluoroheptanoic acid (known as PFHpA), 
perfluorononanoic acid (known as PFNA), perfluorodecanoic acid (known as PFDA) and 4,8 -dioxia-3H-
perfluorononanoic acid (known as ADONA).  There can be no assurance as to the timing of the release of such 
recommendations.  The establishment of a Hazard Index (a tool that assesses the cumulative health effect) for 
aggregate levels of the foregoing compounds other than ADONA is also included in the EPA’s March 14, 
2023 regulatory proposal, which is described above. 
The City is required to test its water supplies for PFAS and adopted PFAS testing in 2018.   Water 
produced by the Water System meets all State and federal water quality standards. Further, the CGTS system 
described above provides a robust barrier to extract PFAS to a non-detect level. 
The City does not anticipate that implementation of the lowered PFAS RL by the Division will have a 
material adverse effect on the operation of the Water System or the costs thereof.  The projected operating 
results which are set forth under the caption “WATER SYSTEM FINANCIAL INFORMATION—Projected 
Operating Results and Debt Service Coverage” do not assume significant increases in water treatment or other 
Water System operating costs to meet State regulations relating to PFAS. 
EPA Settlement.  The City receives payments from the EPA pursuant to a settlement, under which the 
City receives reimbursement for costs incurred to treat water pumped from the City’s wells  (the “EPA 
Settlement”).  See the caption “—Water Quality and Treatment—General .”  Pursuant to the EPA Settlement, 
the City received approximately $2.6 million in Fiscal Year 2019 -20, approximately $1.855 million in Fiscal 
Year 2020 -21, approximately $700,000  in Fiscal Year 2021 -22, and approximately $7,000 in Fiscal Year 
2022-23.  The City expects to receive retroactive payments under the EPA Settlement for Fiscal Year 2022 -23 
from WQA in Fiscal Year 2023 -24.  The City’s EPA Settlement Payments were historically made by WQA 
using payments received from EPA.  EPA is currently negotiating an agreement for distribution of future 
settlement payments with the State Department of Toxic Substances Control (“DTSC”); once that agreement is 
finalized, the EPA Settlement payments will be made through DTSC instead of WQA.  The City’s assumptions 
regarding the amount and timing of future EPA Settlement payments are described in footnote 1 to Table 14; 
however, the City cannot provide any assurance that payments under the EPA Settlement will be made in the 
amounts or at the times described in this Official Statement.  
PFAS Settlement.  The City is a plaintiff to proposed class action settlements referred to as In re 
Aqueous Film-Forming Foam (“AFFF”) MDL No. 2873 (the “PFAS Settlements”), relating to groundwater 
contamination allegedly caused by the 3M Company and certain companies owned or controlled by the 
DuPont corporation.  Pursuant to the PFAS Settlements, the City should receive payments totaling 
approximately $7.2 million over the next 10 years (after payment of contingency legal fees), with the first, and 
largest, payment to be made as soon as the summer of 2024.  The litigation to which the settlement relates, 
which is commonly referred to as the AFFF Multi -District Litigation  (the “MDL Litigation”), involves 
hundreds of cases related to Aqueous Film -Forming Foams (AFFF) and their alleged contamination of 
groundwater with harmful substances.   
The PFAS Settlement involves only 3M and DuPont; other defendants to the MDL Litigation are not 
parties to the PFAS Settlements and the City could receive settlement payments or recovery of damages in 
connection with the cases pending against those parties in the future.  However, the PFAS Settlements would 
resolve approximately 75% of the City’s claims in the MDL Litigation.  The PFAS settlements with DuPont 
and 3M have received final approval from the judge in the MDL Litigation.  The City cannot guarantee that the 
City will receive the settlement payments in the amounts, or at the times, described above.  Further, the City 
can provide no assurance that any other defendants in the MDL Litigation will enter into settlement 
agreements or that the City will receive any further payments as a result of the MDL Litigation.  The 
projections set forth in Table 14 do not assume the receipt of settlement payments under the PFAS Settlements  
and the City can provide no assurance as to the amount and timing of any such payments.  
29 
 
Drought Declarations 
State Orders .  On January 17, 2014, the Governor declared a drought state of emergency (the 
“Declaration”) with immediate effect.  The Declaration included an order encouraging local urban water 
suppliers, including the City, to implement their local water shortage contingency plans; the City’s plan is 
discussed under the subcaption “ — Water Shortage Contingency Plan .”  On April 7, 2017, after significant 
improvement in water supply conditions across California, the Governor issued Executive Order B- 40-17, 
which rescinded mandatory conservation measures for most California counties (including the County).   
In 2018, the California Governor signed Senate Bill 606 and Assembly Bill 1668 into law.  These bills 
relate to water conservation and drought planning and empower DWR and the State of California Water 
Resources Control Board to adopt long -term standards for the following: (i) indoor residential water use; (ii) 
outdoor residential water use; (iii) commercial, industrial and institutional water use for landscape irrigation; 
and (iv) water loss.  The indoor water use standard has been defined as 55 gallons per person per day 
(“GPCD”) until January 2025; the standard will decrease over time to 50 GPCD in January 2030.  Standards 
for outdoor residential water use and commercial, industrial and institutional water use for landscape irrigation 
are still being developed.  Urban water suppliers will be required to stay within annual water budgets, based on 
these standards, for their service areas. 
The City is unable to predict the effect on the Water System of the implementation of Senate Bill 606 
and Assembly Bill 1668 or any future legislation with respect to water conservation.  However, the City is 
meeting and believes that it will continue to be able to meet the 55 GPCD standard for indoor water use, along 
with annual State- mandated water use efficiency standards, based on the City’s current water demands and 
ongoing efforts to encourage conservation, as described below under the subcaption “ — Water Shortage 
Contingency Plan.”  The City also has a tiered rate structure for residential customers that further promotes 
water use efficiency by charging a higher rate for water use in excess of maximum amounts within each tier.  
See the caption “—Water System Rates and Charges — Current Rates and Charges.” 
On October 19, 2021, the Governor declared a Statewide drought state of emergency and requested 
that all water users voluntarily reduce water use by 15%.  On March 28, 2022, the Governor requested that all 
water users voluntarily reduce water use by 20%.  These declarations encouraged water agencies to draw upon 
supplies other than groundwater and to implement their water shortage contingency plans and authorized the 
State Water Resources Control Board (the “SWRCB”) to adopt regulations that prohibit wasteful water use 
(such as the use of potable water to wash paved surfaces or to irrigate landscaping during the two days 
following rainfall).  Such regulations went into effect on June 10, 2022.   
In order to achieve compliance with the SWRCB’s June 10, 2022 regulations, the City is currently 
implementing Stage 2 of its water shortage contingency plan.  The City believes that it has significant water 
supplies and storage and is well positioned to respond to both drought and regulatory requirements.  At this 
time, the City does not foresee a water supply shortage .  See the subcaption “— Water Shortage Contingency 
Plan.”  There can be no assurance that subsequent State declarations will not impose mandatory water use 
restrictions should dry conditions return in future years.  The City notes that the State experienced significant 
precipitation in the winter and early spring of 2023. 
Water Shortage Contingency Plan .  On September 15, 2021, the City adopted Ordinance No.  2214, 
which added water conservation regulations to the City’s municipal code.  The City’s water shortage 
contingency plan, which is referred to as the Water Conservation Plan and which meets State guidelines for 
such plans, calls for the City to respond to a water shortage in stages as follows: 
• Stage 1: Declaring a Stage 1 Drought Emergency demonstrates the need to 
reduce the City’s water demand by a maximum of 10% and includes voluntary water use 
restrictions, such as restricting watering outdoors without the use of drip irrigation or micro -
spray systems to three times per week.  
30 
 
• Stage 2: Declaring a Stage 2 Drought Emergency demonstrates the need to 
reduce the City’s water demand between 11% and 20% and includes mandatory water use 
restrictions, such as restricting watering outdoors without the use of drip irrigation or micro -
spray systems to two times per week, prohibiting the installation of non- drip irrigation or 
micro-spray systems used in outdoor watering in new commercial and residential 
developments requesting water service and prohibiting the use of potable water to irrigate 
ornamental turf on public street medians.  
• Stage 3: Declaring a Stage 3 Drought Emergency demonstrates the need to 
reduce the City’s water demand between 21% and 30% and includes mandatory water use 
restrictions, such as restricting watering outdoors without the use of drip irrigation or micro -
spray systems to one time per week, restricting the filling of swimming pools and hot stubs to 
not more than one time per week, restricting the use of potable water from fire hydrants to 
firefighting and emergency and public health related activities, prohibiting the installation of 
non-drip irrigation or micro -spray systems used in outdoor watering in new commercial and 
residential developments requesting water service, prohibiting the use of potable water to 
irrigate ornamental turf on public street medians and prohibiting the washing of vehicles 
unless performed at commercial car wash facilities. 
• Stage 4: Declaring a Stage 4 Drought Emergency demonstrates the need to 
reduce the City’s water demand between 31% and 40% and includes mandatory water use 
restrictions such as completely prohibiting watering outdoors without the use of drip 
irrigation or micro -spray systems in addition to the other water use  restrictions applicable 
under Stage 3. 
• Stage 5: Declaring a Stage 5 Drought Emergency demonstrates the need to 
reduce the City’s water demand between 41% and 50% and includes the other water use 
restrictions described in the City’s water contingency plan. 
• Stage 6: Declaring a Stage 6 Drought Emergency demonstrates the need to 
reduce the City’s water demand by more than 60% and includes the other water use 
restrictions described in the City’s water contingency plan. 
The City is empowered to enforce its water shortage contingency plan with successively increasing 
fines, the installation of flow restriction devices and the termination of water service. 
The projected Water System operating results that are set forth under the caption “WATER SYSTEM 
FINANCIAL INFORMATION—Projected Operating Results and Debt Service Coverage” do not reflect the 
implementation of mandatory levels of the City’s water contingency plan.  The City does not currently expect 
that the implementation of the City’s water contingency plan in the future will have a material adverse effect 
on its ability to pay the Series 2024 Installment Payments from Net Revenues.  As discussed under the caption 
“—Water System Rates and Charges,” the City’s rate structure consists of variable and fixed rate components.  
Decreased water consumption is partially offset by a decrease in related variable costs, while fixed water 
charges largely cover the Water System’s fixed operating and maintenance costs.  In addition, the City has 
covenanted to set Water System rates and charges in an amount that it expects to be sufficient to produce Net 
Revenues of at least 120% of Debt Service, as further described under the caption “SECURITY FOR THE 
BONDS— Additional Parity Bonds and Contracts.” 
It is likely that a water shortage will arise again in the future.  In such event legal issues exist as to 
whether different California Water Code provisions or State regulations will be invoked to manage the 
allocation of water.  Any curtailment pursuant to State orders could necessitate an increase in the City’s water 
rates to Water System customers.  See the caption “CONSTITUTIONAL LIMITATIONS ON  
31 
 
APPROPRIATIONS AND CHARGES— Proposition 218” for a discussion of certain restrictions on the City’s 
ability to raise water rates.

Water System Rates and Charges 
General.  The Water System’s rates and charges are set by the City Council and are not subject to the 
jurisdiction of, or regulation by, the California Public Utilities Commission or any other regulatory body.  The 
City is, however, required to comply with the notice, hearing and majority protest provisions of Article XIIID 
of the State Constitution, which is popularly known as Proposition 218, as to Water System rates and charges.  
See the caption “CONSTITUTIONAL LIMITATIONS ON APPROPRIATIONS AND CHARGES—
Proposition 218” for further information with respect to Proposition 218. 
The City Council determines the adequacy of the charge structure for water service in the service area 
after full consideration of expected operations, maintenance, capital costs and capital repayment obligations of 
the Water System.  The City Council sets water rates and charges at a level that it determines is sufficient to 
pay all Operation and Maintenance Costs of the Water System, to make debt service payments and to maintain 
appropriate reserves for the Water System in compliance with the rate covenant set forth in the Installment 
Purchase Agreement. 
The 2024 Project includes the replacement of all of the City’s existing water meters, which are past 
their useful life.  The existing meters are Single- jet AMR meters. The new meters are expected to use 
ultrasonic technology, which holds its accuracy longer than standard meters. Additionally, the proposed 
replacement meters will provide instant data to the City and water users, including notification of leaks, which 
will reduce water waste.  See the caption “THE 2024 PROJECT” for a further discussion regarding the 2024 
Project. 
Adopted Water Rates and Charges .  On December  20, 2023, the City adopted rates (the “Adopted 
Rate Plan”) effective February  1, 2024 in accordance with the provisions of Proposition 218.  The Adopted 
Rate Plan includes increases of up to 32% effective February  1, 2024, 8% effective July  1, 2024, 8% effective  
35 
 
July 1, 2025, 7% effective July  1, 2026 and 8% effective July  1, 2027 .  On February, 21, 2024, the City 
Council approved the implementation of water rates and charges consistent with the Adopted Rate Plan, as 
follows:  increases of 28% effective March 1, 2024, 8% effective July 1, 2024, 7% effective July 1, 2025, 7% 
effective July 1, 2026 and 6% effective July  1, 2027.  The water rates described in the following tables reflect 
the implementation of the City Council’s February 21, 2024 direction.  There can be no assurance that the City 
Council will not repeal or modify the rates that are set forth in the Adopted Rate Plan in the future or that the 
City’s ratepayers will not approve an initiative to repeal or modify any increase in water rates and charges 
approved by the City Council.  
The Adopted Rate Plan includes a monthly service charge and a water consumption charge.  The 
water rates for the Adopted Rate Plan are set forth below:

Water System Collection Procedures 
The City is currently transitioning from a bimonthly to a monthly billing cycle for Water System 
customers.  The transition was completed for all customers by the end of February 2024.  At that time, a 
consolidated bill which includes wastewater and other utility charges is sent to customers every month for 
services rendered in the prior month.  All accounts that remain unpaid on the 34th day following the delivery 
of an invoice incur a penalty of the lesser of 10% of the amount due or $10, and a notice is mailed to the billing 
and service address requiring payment within 48 days.  If payment is not received by the 83rd day following 
the original delivery of an invoice, a two weeks -notice of shutoff is posted at the service address and a final 
notice is mailed to the billing address.  If payment is not received by the second week following the posting of 
the shutoff notice, service is shut off.  Accounts that have service discontinued without an alternate payment 
plan are not reconnected without full payment of the delinquent bill and all penalties, including a tagging fee, a 
reinstatement fee, and certain other charges.  
37 
 
As of December 31, 2023, less than 3% of the Water System’s outstanding billings were delinquent, 
which is slightly higher than historical averages of approximately 2.81%.  In January 2022, all delinquent 
accounts were brought to a zero balance as a result of payments from the State Water Resources Control Board 
COVID-19 Coronavirus State and Local Fiscal Recovery Fund, in the total amount of $577,276.  This figure  
reflects an aging report and reflects the total of all bills not paid during prior Fiscal Years, including amounts 
shown as delinquent relating to the COVID -19 outbreak (during which the City did not shut off water for 
delinquent accounts through June 2023) and delinquencies being paid over time under customer payment 
plans.

WATER SYSTEM FINANCIAL INFORMATION 
Available Cash 
As of June 30, 2023, the Water System had approximately $ 15,955,496 in available cash and 
investments.   
Management Discussion of Historic Operating Results and Debt Service Coverage 
As discussed in Note 17 to the City’s audited financial statements for Fiscal Year 2019 -20, the City 
restated the net position of the business -type activities and major enterprise fund at July 1, 2019.  The 
beginning net position of the business -type activities in the government- wide financial statements and of the 
Water Utility Enterprise Fund in the proprietary fund financial statements have been restated (decreased) by 
$413,040 to correct the duplicate reporting of a grant reimbursement receivable in the prior year.  Table 13 
reflects this restatement. 
GASB Statement No. 34 requires consolidated government-wide statements with a single Statement of 
Net Assets and a single Statement of Activities. As a general rule, the assets and liabilities of internal service 
funds will be eliminated, for the most part, in the process of consolidation. Any remaining balances that are not 
eliminated normally will be reported in the  appropriate business -type or governmental.  In accordance with 
this procedure, the net position of the Water Fund was adjusted (decreased) to reflect the consolidation to the 
Internal Service Fund by the following amounts:  $ 4,005,039 as of June 30, 2019, $5,105,729 as of June 30, 
2020, $5,778,933 as of June 30, 2021, $ 6,415,914 as of June 30, 2022, and $6,829,215 as of as of June 30, 
2023.   
In the City’s audited financial statement for the year ended June 30, 2023, the beginning net position 
of the Water Utility Enterprise Fund was restated to reallocate the deferred outflows and inflows of the City’s 
pension and OPEB costs and liabilities, resulting in a net increase in the beginning net position of $1,523,021.

(8) Provides for the costs associated with water remediation activities for the Dual Barrier treatment facility, which is operated for use by Well 
Nos. 9, 12, and 15 for removal of volatile organic compounds not removed by the Well No. 12 Air Stripper; as indicated by the

Organic Compounds (VOC) in the groundwater from the San Gabriel Valley Water Basin; ground water remediation for Wells No. 1,  No. 3, 
No. 10 and Fern Well for volatile organic compounds (VOC) and future contaminants that may be found in the system.  This includes the 
operation and maintenance of the Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) treatment system for these wells. This treatment plant has a capacity of 
4,500 gallons per minute and requires continual scheduled maintenance and monitoring; provides for the costs associated with ground water 
remediation for Well No. 5 for volatile organic compounds that may be found in the groundwater.  Decrease in Fiscal Year 2021 -22 reflects
